pages.title: Project Atlas: Real-time Dashboards
pages.markdown:
## Overview
Project Atlas delivers real-time dashboards for Meridian customers. The goal is to let analysts build live views over streaming event data without writing SQL.

## Goals
- Sub second dashboard refresh for the median workspace
- Support for at least 50 concurrent widgets per dashboard
- A drag and drop widget editor in the frontend

## Non-goals
- We are not building a full BI suite in this phase
- Mobile layouts are deferred to a later milestone

## Technical approach
The backend reads from the new query engine described in RFC: Query Engine Architecture. We hold a p99 latency target of 200ms for cached queries. The frontend uses a React widget grid backed by a websocket channel for live updates.

## Open questions
- How do we handle very large result sets in the browser
- Do we need server side aggregation for the heaviest widgets

## Status
The team is in an active push toward the Q3 launch. Daily progress is tracked in Atlas Standup Notes Week of June 22. The current blocker is index performance on the events table, which is being addressed in Database Indexing Improvements.

## Owners
- Backend: Priya
- Frontend: Marcus
- Product: Dana

pages.title: Project Helios: Billing Revamp
pages.markdown:
## Summary
Project Helios replaces our legacy billing scripts with a managed billing service. Today invoices are generated by a nightly cron that is fragile and hard to audit.

## Motivation
- Finance spends roughly two days each month reconciling invoices
- We cannot currently support usage based add ons
- The Pro tier price of $49 per seat per month is hardcoded in three places

## Plan
- Move seat counting into a single billing service
- Emit a billing event for every seat change
- Reconcile against the events table once per hour

## Risks
- Migrating existing customers without double charging
- Handling proration for mid cycle seat changes

## Dependencies
Helios depends on the events pipeline from Project Nimbus. If Nimbus stays blocked we will need a temporary read path against the old pipeline.

## Timeline
Target completion is end of Q3. The first milestone is a shadow run where Helios computes invoices in parallel with the old system for one full billing cycle.

pages.title: Project Nimbus: Data Pipeline Rebuild
pages.markdown:
## Context
Nimbus rebuilds our ingestion pipeline so that customer events land in Postgres within five seconds of arrival. The old pipeline batches every fifteen minutes, which is too slow for real-time dashboards.

## Why this is blocked
We are blocked on a decision about the message broker. The infra team wants Kafka but the current Meridian scale does not justify the operational cost. A lighter option is Postgres logical replication plus a small queue.

## Requirements
- At least once delivery for every event
- Backpressure when downstream is slow
- Replay from the last 7 days

## Current thinking
We will start with a single writer that appends to the events table and let dashboards read directly. This keeps the architecture simple and unblocks Project Atlas. We can revisit Kafka when ingestion exceeds 10,000 events per second.

## Next step
Finalize the broker decision in the next architecture sync. Until then Nimbus stays blocked and Atlas reads from the legacy path.

pages.title: Project Orion: ML Anomaly Detection
pages.markdown:
## Vision
Orion adds automatic anomaly detection on top of customer metrics. When a metric deviates from its expected band we surface an alert in the dashboard.

## Approach
- Start with a simple seasonal model per metric
- Compute a rolling baseline over a 28 day window
- Flag points outside three standard deviations

## Why not deep learning yet
Most Meridian customers have fewer than two years of history. A heavy model would overfit. We prefer an interpretable baseline that analysts can trust and tune.

## Data needs
Orion reads from the same events table as Atlas. It will also reuse the embeddings work described in Embeddings and Vector Search Design for grouping similar metrics.

## Success criteria
- Fewer than one false alert per metric per week
- Detection within one hour of a real anomaly

## Status
Not started. This is parked behind Atlas and Helios for the quarter and will be reconsidered during Q4 planning.

pages.title: Database Indexing Improvements
pages.markdown:
## Problem
Dashboard queries against the events table slowed down as data grew. The worst query, a per customer aggregation over the last 30 days, took 1.2 seconds at p95. This breaks the Atlas latency budget.

## Investigation
We traced the slow query to a sequential scan on events. The planner could not use the existing single column index on customer_id because most queries also filter on event_time.

## Fix
We added a composite index on (customer_id, event_time). After the change the same query dropped from 1.2 seconds to 80ms at p95. We also added a partial index for the small set of high volume customers.

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_events_customer_time
  ON events (customer_id, event_time DESC)
```

## Results
- p95 for the aggregation query: 80ms
- Index build took 4 minutes on the replica
- No measurable write slowdown at current volume

## Follow up
This directly unblocks Project Atlas. Next we will review the caching layer so repeated dashboard loads avoid the database entirely.

pages.title: Frontend Bundle Size Reduction
pages.markdown:
## Why
The Meridian web app ships a 2.3 MB JavaScript bundle. On a typical analyst connection the dashboard takes over four seconds to become interactive. This hurts the Atlas experience.

## Findings
- The charting library accounts for 900 KB on its own
- We import the entire icon set but use about forty icons
- Date utilities are bundled twice through two transitive dependencies

## Plan
- Lazy load the charting library only on dashboard routes
- Switch to per icon imports
- Deduplicate the date library through a single resolution

## Expected impact
Initial estimates suggest we can cut the bundle to under 1 MB and bring time to interactive under two seconds. This pairs with the backend work in Database Indexing Improvements to make the whole Atlas load feel fast.

## Status
Lazy loading is merged. Icon cleanup is in review. The date dedupe needs a dependency upgrade that is being tested.

pages.title: RFC: Query Engine Architecture
pages.markdown:
## Summary
This RFC proposes the query engine that powers Atlas dashboards. The engine accepts a declarative query, plans it, and returns aggregated results over the events table.

## Requirements
- A p99 latency target of 200ms for cached queries
- p99 under 800ms for cold queries
- Results must be consistent within five seconds of ingestion

## Design
The engine compiles each dashboard widget into a parameterized SQL query against Postgres. Frequently used queries are cached in Redis. A background worker warms the cache for active dashboards.

## Why Postgres
We rely on relational joins between events, customers, and seats. See RFC: Choosing Postgres over DynamoDB for the full comparison.

## Caching
The caching layer is specified separately in Caching Layer Design. The short version is a 60 second TTL with tag based invalidation when new events land.

## Alternatives considered
- A dedicated OLAP store such as ClickHouse. Rejected for now due to operational cost at our scale.
- Precomputed rollups only. Rejected because analysts need ad hoc filters.

## Decision
Approved. The 200ms p99 target is the primary success metric for the engine.

pages.title: RFC: Choosing Postgres over DynamoDB
pages.markdown:
## Context
We needed a primary datastore for events, customers, seats, and billing records. The two finalists were Postgres and DynamoDB.

## Decision
We chose Postgres over DynamoDB because our access patterns are heavily relational. Dashboards join events against customer and seat tables, and billing needs transactional consistency across several tables.

## Comparison
- Relational queries: Postgres supports the joins and ad hoc aggregations we need. DynamoDB would force us to denormalize and maintain many derived tables.
- Transactions: Postgres gives us multi row transactions out of the box. This matters for Project Helios billing.
- Operational familiarity: the team already runs Postgres in production.
- Scale: DynamoDB scales further, but we are far from the point where a single Postgres primary with read replicas runs out of room.

## Tradeoffs
Postgres requires us to manage our own sharding story eventually. We accept that risk because relational query needs dominate today. We will revisit if write volume exceeds 10,000 events per second.

## Decision owners
Approved by the architecture group on May 22.

pages.title: Security Review: Q2 2026
pages.markdown:
## Scope
This review covered the public API, the dashboard web app, and the billing service. It was conducted over two weeks by an internal team plus one external reviewer.

## Findings
- High: API keys were logged in plaintext in one debug code path. Fixed during the review.
- Medium: the password reset token did not expire. We now expire reset tokens after 30 minutes.
- Medium: missing rate limiting on the login endpoint. Tracked in Rate Limiting Design.
- Low: verbose error messages leaked stack traces in non production environments only.

## Highest priority item
The most serious issue was plaintext API keys in logs. We rotated all affected keys and added a log scrubber that redacts any value matching the key format.

## Controls added
- Mandatory key rotation every 90 days
- Secret scanning in continuous integration
- A quarterly review cadence going forward

## Outcome
No evidence of exploitation was found. All high and medium findings are resolved or tracked. The next review is scheduled for Q3.

pages.title: API Versioning Strategy
pages.markdown:
## Problem
The Meridian public API has no versioning scheme. As Atlas adds new dashboard endpoints we risk breaking existing customer integrations.

## Decision
We will use header based versioning. Clients send an Accept-Version header such as Accept-Version: 2026-06-01. When the header is absent we default to the oldest supported version so existing integrations keep working.

## Why not URL versioning
We considered putting the version in the path like /v2/query. We rejected it because it encourages large breaking jumps and makes incremental changes awkward. Header based dated versions let us evolve gradually.

## Rules
- Each version is a date string
- We support at least the last three dated versions
- Breaking changes require a new dated version and a migration note

## Rollout
The gateway reads the header and routes to the correct handler. Undated requests are logged so we can find customers who need to pin a version before we drop the default. This work is part of the active Atlas push toward Q3.

pages.title: Caching Layer Design
pages.markdown:
## Goal
Reduce load on Postgres and hit the Atlas p99 latency target of 200ms by caching dashboard query results.

## Design
We cache compiled query results in Redis. Each cache key is a hash of the query plan and its parameters. Entries use a 60 second TTL so dashboards stay fresh without hammering the database.

## Invalidation
A TTL alone can serve slightly stale data. For widgets that need freshness we tag cache entries by customer. When new events land for a customer we invalidate that customer tag so the next read recomputes.

## Cache warming
A background worker refreshes the cache for dashboards that were viewed in the last fifteen minutes. This keeps the common case warm and within budget.

## Open questions
- Do we need a second tier in process cache for the hottest queries
- How do we cap memory use as the number of active dashboards grows

## Relationship to other work
This layer sits in front of the query engine from RFC: Query Engine Architecture and depends on the faster reads from Database Indexing Improvements.

pages.title: Rate Limiting Design
pages.markdown:
## Motivation
The security review flagged that several endpoints have no rate limiting. Without it a single client can exhaust database connections or brute force the login endpoint.

## Proposed limits
- Public API: 100 requests per minute per API key
- Login endpoint: 10 attempts per minute per IP address
- Export endpoint: 5 requests per minute per workspace

## Algorithm
We will use a token bucket stored in Redis. Each key has a bucket that refills at a steady rate. When the bucket is empty we return a 429 with a Retry-After header.

## Response contract
- Status 429 Too Many Requests
- Retry-After in seconds
- A header showing remaining quota in the current window

## Rollout plan
- Start in shadow mode and only log when a limit would trip
- Tune the numbers using one week of real traffic
- Enforce once we confirm normal customers stay well under the limits

## Status
Not started. This is queued behind the Atlas launch but is a tracked follow up from Security Review: Q2 2026.

pages.title: Postmortem: June 12 Outage
pages.markdown:
## Summary
On June 12 the Meridian dashboard was unavailable for 47 minutes between 14:03 and 14:50 UTC. Customers could not load any dashboard during this window. No data was lost.

## Impact
- Dashboard availability dropped to zero for 47 minutes
- The public API returned errors for roughly 60 percent of requests
- Billing and ingestion were unaffected

## Root cause
The outage was caused by connection pool exhaustion. A deploy lowered the pool size while a slow analytics query held connections open far longer than expected. New requests could not acquire a connection and timed out.

## Timeline
- 14:03 error rate spikes after the deploy
- 14:12 on call paged
- 14:31 connection pool exhaustion identified
- 14:50 pool size restored and the slow query killed

## What went well
- Alerting fired within two minutes
- No data loss

## Action items
- Add a guardrail so deploys cannot lower the pool below a floor
- Add a statement timeout for analytics queries
- Surface connection pool saturation on the main dashboard

These items connect to the caching and indexing work that reduces long running queries.

pages.title: Embeddings and Vector Search Design
pages.markdown:
## Purpose
This design covers semantic search across Meridian dashboards, metrics, and saved queries. Today search is keyword only, so analysts miss results that use different wording.

## Approach
We embed each searchable object into a vector and store it alongside the row in Postgres using the pgvector extension. At query time we embed the search string and run a nearest neighbor lookup.

## Model choice
We use a 1536 dimension embedding model. The dimension is a balance between quality and storage cost. Each vector is about 6 KB, which is acceptable for our current object count.

## Indexing
We build an approximate nearest neighbor index so search stays fast as the catalog grows. We will start with an IVFFlat index and revisit HNSW if recall is not good enough.

## Connection to Orion
Project Orion can reuse these embeddings to group similar metrics for anomaly detection. Sharing the embedding pipeline avoids duplicate infrastructure.

## Open questions
- How often do we re embed when objects change
- Do we need a separate model for short metric names versus long descriptions

pages.title: Q3 Planning Meeting
pages.markdown:
## Attendees
Dana (Product), Priya (Backend lead), Marcus (Frontend lead), Sam (Infra), Lena (CEO)

## Theme
The quarter is focused on shipping Project Atlas and finishing Project Helios billing.

## Decisions
- Atlas is the top priority for Q3 and ships by the end of the quarter
- Project Orion stays parked until Q4
- We will hold the Pro tier price at $49 per seat per month for the rest of the year

## Action items
- Priya: land the composite index work and unblock Atlas query latency
- Marcus: cut the dashboard bundle under 1 MB before launch
- Sam: finalize the message broker decision so Nimbus can unblock
- Dana: write the Atlas launch announcement and update the roadmap
- Lena: review pricing with finance and confirm no change for existing customers

## Risks raised
- Nimbus is still blocked and Atlas depends on its event freshness
- The security review left rate limiting unimplemented

## Next check in
Weekly Atlas standups will track progress. We meet again for a mid quarter review in early August.

pages.title: Atlas Standup Notes Week of June 22
pages.markdown:
## Monday June 22
- Priya: composite index deployed to the replica, validating query plans
- Marcus: icon import cleanup in review
- Dana: drafting the launch checklist

## Tuesday June 23
- Priya: index live in production, aggregation query now at 80ms p95
- Marcus: lazy loading the chart library merged
- Sam: leaning toward Postgres logical replication instead of Kafka for Nimbus

## Wednesday June 24
- Priya: starting on cache warming for active dashboards
- Marcus: time to interactive down to 2.4 seconds, chasing the date library dedupe
- Dana: API versioning header agreed, see API Versioning Strategy

## Blockers
- Nimbus broker decision still open, target is the next architecture sync
- We need a load test before we trust the 200ms p99 target under real traffic

## Notes
Momentum is good this week. The combination of the new index and the caching layer is bringing dashboard loads into budget.

pages.title: Customer Call: Northwind Analytics
pages.markdown:
## Attendees
Dana and Priya from Meridian. Two analysts and one engineering manager from Northwind Analytics.

## Context
Northwind is a design partner on the Pro tier. They have 25 seats and are one of our larger accounts.

## What they love
- The new dashboards are fast and easy to share
- Seat based pricing is predictable for their finance team

## Pain points
- They badly want CSV export from any dashboard. This came up three times.
- They hit slow loads before the recent indexing fix
- They want SSO before they expand to more teams

## Churn risk
Northwind is not at immediate churn risk, but the missing CSV export is blocking a wider rollout inside their company. If we do not ship export this quarter they will delay expanding seats.

## Action items
- Dana: add CSV export to the roadmap as a near term item
- Priya: confirm the indexing fix resolved their slow loads
- Dana: scope SSO for a future quarter

pages.title: Engineering Retro: May
pages.markdown:
## What went well
- The Postgres decision gave us a clear foundation
- The query engine RFC landed with strong agreement
- On call load was lighter than April

## What did not go well
- We caught a connection pool scare in staging that nearly reached production
- Nimbus stayed blocked the whole month
- We underestimated the frontend bundle work

## What we learned
- Deploys need guardrails around shared resources like the connection pool
- Blocked projects need a clear owner to drive the unblocking decision

## Action items
- Add deploy guardrails for the connection pool
- Assign Sam as the single owner of the Nimbus broker decision
- Budget real time for performance work in the next planning cycle

## Metrics for the month
- Deploys to production: 41 with one rollback
- Median pull request review time stayed under one business day
- No customer facing incidents reached the postmortem bar

## Shout outs
- Priya for steady backend progress
- Marcus for steady progress on the editor
- Sam for keeping the staging environment healthy during a busy month

pages.title: Engineering Onboarding Guide
pages.markdown:
## Welcome to Meridian Engineering
This guide gets a new engineer productive in the first week.

## Day one
- Get access to the codebase and the staging environment
- Install Postgres locally and load the sample dataset
- Read RFC: Query Engine Architecture and RFC: Choosing Postgres over DynamoDB

## Our stack
- Postgres is our primary datastore
- Redis is used for caching and rate limiting
- The web app is React. The backend is a TypeScript service.

## How we work
- Small pull requests reviewed within one business day
- Every change ships behind a feature flag when it touches customer surfaces
- We write a short design doc for anything that takes more than a week

## On call
- One week rotation
- Page only for customer facing outages
- Always write a postmortem for any incident, see Postmortem: June 12 Outage as an example

## Useful docs
- The plan for the quarter lives in Q3 Roadmap
- Security practices are in Security Review: Q2 2026

## First task
Your onboarding buddy will assign a starter bug. The goal is to ship something small to production by the end of week one.

pages.title: Pricing Decision: Pro Tier
pages.markdown:
## Decision
We will price the Pro tier at $49 per seat per month, billed annually or month to month. This was confirmed at the Q3 Planning Meeting and holds for the rest of the year.

## Tiers
- Free: up to 1,000 events per day and a single dashboard
- Pro: $49 per seat per month with unlimited dashboards and 30 day history
- Enterprise: custom pricing with SSO, longer retention, and a dedicated success manager

## Why $49
We benchmarked three competitors in the B2B analytics space. Their comparable tiers ranged from $40 to $65 per seat. We chose $49 to sit just below the middle while signaling that Meridian is a serious product.

## Why per seat
Per seat pricing is predictable for the finance teams of our customers. Northwind Analytics specifically called this out as a reason they like Meridian. Usage based add ons are planned through Project Helios but the base price stays per seat.

## Existing customers
No existing customer sees a price change. Any future change requires 60 days notice.

## Open question
Whether to add an annual discount of around 15 percent. Finance is modeling the impact.

pages.title: Q3 Roadmap
pages.markdown:
## Theme for Q3
Ship Project Atlas and finish Project Helios. Hold pricing steady and address the top customer requests.

## Committed
- Project Atlas real-time dashboards launches by end of quarter
- Project Helios billing runs a full shadow cycle
- CSV export ships in response to repeated customer demand
- Rate limiting from the security review goes live

## Planned
- API versioning rolls out with header based dated versions
- The caching layer and indexing work bring dashboard p99 to the 200ms target
- SSO scoping for Enterprise customers

## Parked
- Project Orion anomaly detection moves to Q4
- Mobile dashboard layouts
- A move to a dedicated OLAP store

## Dependencies
Atlas depends on Nimbus unblocking its event freshness. Helios depends on the Nimbus events pipeline as well. The Nimbus broker decision is the single biggest schedule risk this quarter.

## How we measure success
- Atlas adoption by at least half of Pro workspaces in the first month
- Dashboard p99 latency at or under 200ms
- At least two design partners expand seats after CSV export ships

pages.title: Customer Feedback Summary: Q2
pages.markdown:
## Overview
This summarizes customer feedback gathered in Q2 across support tickets, calls, and the in app survey. We collected input from 14 accounts.

## Top requests
1. CSV export from any dashboard. Mentioned by 9 of 14 accounts and the single most requested feature.
2. Single sign on. Requested by larger accounts moving toward wider rollouts.
3. Faster dashboard loads. This is being addressed by the indexing and caching work.

## Themes
- Customers value predictable per seat pricing
- Speed is a feature. Several accounts noticed slow loads before the recent fixes.
- Sharing and collaboration are working well and rarely generate complaints

## Notable quotes
- One analyst said CSV export is the only thing keeping them from rolling Meridian out to their whole team
- Northwind Analytics raised export three separate times on their June call

## Recommended actions
- Prioritize CSV export this quarter
- Scope SSO for Enterprise
- Continue the performance push so no customer waits more than two seconds for a dashboard

## Sentiment
Overall sentiment is positive and trending up since the latest performance improvements.
